name: Hyper (Rust HTTP)
what_it_does: Low-level async HTTP/1 and HTTP/2 library for Rust. Hyper is the HTTP foundation that powers reqwest (HTTP client), Axum (server), and many other Rust HTTP tools. Hyper 1.x provides a minimal, correct, and fast HTTP implementation without built-in routing or higher-level abstractions. Used directly when you need maximum control over HTTP behavior — connection reuse, streaming, protocol details — or when building your own HTTP framework.
use_cases: Build custom HTTP clients for agent tooling with fine-grained control over connection pooling, keep-alive, and HTTP/2 multiplexing
Implement HTTP proxies or forwarders for agent traffic — hyper's low-level body streaming enables efficient proxy implementations
Create custom HTTP server implementations for agent protocols that need non-standard HTTP handling not supported by Axum
Build connection-level HTTP tooling — health checks, load balancers, service mesh data planes — using hyper's primitive abstractions
Power reqwest or other HTTP clients with custom connector implementations for non-standard transport (Unix sockets, named pipes)
not_for: Application-level REST APIs — use Axum (built on hyper) for routing, middleware, and response types without low-level HTTP management
Teams that don't need HTTP control plane features — reqwest wraps hyper with ergonomic defaults for agent HTTP client use cases
Teams unfamiliar with async Rust — hyper 1.x requires significant Rust experience and understanding of Tokio's execution model
best_when: You need direct control over HTTP protocol details — building HTTP proxies, custom clients with specific connection behaviors, or implementing a new HTTP framework in Rust.
avoid_when: You just need HTTP requests or an API server — use reqwest for clients and Axum for servers instead of hyper's low-level primitives.

auth.notes: Library — no external auth. TLS via rustls or openssl via hyper-tls/hyper-rustls.

pricing.notes: MIT-licensed open source from the hyperium organization.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits — self-hosted. Among the fastest HTTP implementations available in any language.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: hyper::Error provides HTTP protocol errors. Connection errors and timeout errors are typed. Documentation covers error types but requires familiarity with HTTP internals.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: HTTP GET/HEAD are idempotent. Idempotency for POST/PUT is application-level. Hyper supports connection reuse for efficient retries.
known_agent_gotchas: Hyper 1.x is a breaking change from 0.14 — many crates (reqwest, axum) support hyper 1.x but third-party crates may still require 0.14; check compatibility before upgrading
Body types in hyper 1.x must implement the Body trait — converting between String, Bytes, and streaming bodies requires explicit type handling that can be verbose
Hyper requires an executor (Tokio runtime) — hyper calls must run inside a Tokio runtime; forgetting to wrap in #[tokio::main] causes 'no runtime found' panics
HTTP/2 connection management is manual — agents must manage h2 frame sizes, flow control, and stream limits or use higher-level libraries that handle these automatically
Hyper's server does not handle routing — every request hits the same service; routing must be implemented via Tower service combinators or by switching to Axum
Body streaming backpressure must be managed explicitly — reading large response bodies without consuming them blocks the connection; always fully consume or drop the body

security_score_components.security_notes: Rust memory safety eliminates buffer overflow and use-after-free vulnerabilities in HTTP parsing. TLS via rustls (memory-safe) or openssl. Well-audited HTTP implementation.
